### Source A 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2fbbec29-f7c8-45dd-a5e1-45fe9877397f)

### Source B
It is a paradox to brand a technological innovation as tainted goods by its very name. ‘Deepfake’ is a victim of its own capabilities. Negative connotations and recent incidents have pigeonholed this innovation in the taboo zone. The rise of deepfake technology has ushered in very interesting possibilities and challenges. This synthetic media, created through sophisticated artificial intelligence algorithms, has begun to infiltrate various sectors, raising intriguing questions about its potential impact on education and employability.

The dawn of deepfake technology introduces a realm of possibilities in education. Imagine medical students engaging in lifelike surgical simulations or language learners participating in authentic conversations. The potential for deepfake to revolutionise training scenarios is vast and could significantly enhance the educational experience. Beyond simulations, deepfake can transport students to historical events through realistic reenactments or facilitate virtual field trips, transcending the boundaries of traditional education. The immersive nature of deepfake content holds the promise of making learning more engaging and memorable.




### Source C 
However, while these potential abuses of the technology are real and concerning that doesn't mean we should turn a blind eye to the technology’s potential when using it responsibly, says Jaime Donally, a well-known immersive learning expert. 

“Typically, when we're hearing about it, it's in terms of the negative – impersonation and giving false claims,” Donally says. “But really, the technology has the power of bringing people from history alive through old images that we have using AI.” 

Donally, a former math teacher and instructional technologist, has written about how a type of deep fake technology called deep nostalgia technology that went viral in 2021 can allow students to form a stronger connection with the past and their personal family heritage. The technology, available on the MyHeritage app, allows images to uploaded that are then turned into short animations thanks to AI technology. 

Here are some of the ways in which teachers can utilize deep fake technology in the classroom utilizing the MyHeritage app.
Teachers have used the deep fake technology in the My Heritage app to bring historical figures such as Amelia Earhart and Albert Einstein to life. One teacher Donally has communicated with used an animation of Frederick Douglass (above) to help students connect with Douglass’ famous 1852 speech about the meaning of the Fourth of July to Black enslaved Americans. Another teacher has plans to use the app to have students interview a historical figure and create dialogue for them, then match the dialogue to the animation.

Donally herself has paired animations she's created with other types of immersive technology. “I layered it on top in augmented reality,” she says. “When you scan the photo of my grandfather, it all came to life. And it became something that was much more relevant to see in your real-world space.” 

With proper supervision, students can use the technology to animate images of family members or local historical figures, and can experiment with augmented reality (AR) in the process. 
“It makes you want to learn more,” Donally says of animations created using deep fake technology. “It drives you into kind of the history and understanding a bit more, and I think it also helps you identify who you are in that process.” 

### Source D 
Education platforms are harnessing deepfake technology to create AI tutors that provide customised support to students. Rather than a generic video lecture, each learner can get tailored instruction and feedback from a virtual tutor who speaks their language and adjusts to their level.

For example, Anthropic built Claude, an AI assistant designed specifically for education. Claude can answer students’ natural language questions, explain concepts clearly, and identify knowledge gaps.

Such AI tutors make learning more effective, accessible, and inclusive. Students feel like they have an expert guide helping them master new skills and material.

AI and deepfake technology have enormous potential to enhance workforce training and education in immersive new ways too.
As Drew Rose, CSO and founder of cybersecurity firm Living Security, explains, “educators can leverage deepfakes to create immersive learning experiences. For instance, a history lesson might feature a ‘guest appearance’ by a historical figure, or a science lesson might have a renowned scientist explaining complex concepts.”
Ivana Bartoletti, privacy and data protection expert at Wipro and author of An Artificial Revolution – On Power, Politics and AI envisions similar applications.

“Deepfake technologies could provide an easier and less expensive way to train and visualise,” she says.
“Students of medicine and nursing currently train with animatronic robots. They are expensive and require special control rooms. Generative AI and augmented or virtual reality headsets or practice rooms will be cheaper and allow for the generalisation, if not the gamification, of simulation.”

Medical students could gain experience diagnosing and treating simulated patients, while business students could practice high-stakes scenarios like negotiations without real-world consequences.
These immersive, gamified environments enabled by AI and deepfakes also have vast potential for corporate training.

Bartoletti notes, “A similar use case could be made for other types of learning that require risky and skill-based experiences. The Air Force uses AI as adversaries in flight simulators, and humans have not beaten the best AIs since 2015.

### Possible Responses

#### Argument for Incremental Change

- Deepfakes are an extension/evolution of existing technologies like photoshop, video editing etc. $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simply enhancing/automating existing capabilities rather than introducing entirely new ones $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Other Relevant Points

- Ethical concerns around deepfakes (e.g. consent, privacy, misinformation) $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regulatory challenges and need for guidelines $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>
A well-balanced response considering both perspectives is expected. Credit should be given for well-substantiated examples and arguments from either side.
</note>

### Source A: Killer Robots: New UN Report Urges Treaty by 2026
The UN secretary-general has called for states to conclude a new international treaty by 2026 to prohibit weapons systems without human control or oversight and that cannot be used in compliance with international humanitarian law. The treaty should regulate all types of autonomous weapons systems. The report reflects 58 submissions from over 73 countries and 33 submissions from the International Committee of the Red Cross and civil society groups. The UN General Assembly is considered a venue for inclusive discussions on autonomous weapons systems, considering international peace and security concerns.

### Source B: Operation PEACE STORM: The Role of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles and Autonomous Weapons in Libya’s Offensive Strategy
On 27 March 2020, the Prime Minister of Libya, Faiez Serraj, announced the commencement of Operation PEACE STORM, which moved GNA-AF to the offensive along the coastal littoral. The combination of the Gabya-class frigates and Korkut short-range air defence systems provided a capability to place a mobile air defence bubble around GNA-AF ground units, which took Hafter Affiliated Forces (HAF) air assets out of the military equation. Libya classifies HAF as a terrorist rebel organization. The enhanced operational intelligence capability included Turkish-operated signal intelligence and the intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance provided by Bayraktar TB-2 and probably TAI Anka S unmanned combat aerial vehicles. This allowed for the development of an asymmetrical war of attrition designed to degrade HAF ground unit capability. The GNA-AF breakout of Tripoli was supported with Firtina T155 155 mm self-propelled guns and T-122 Sakarya multi-launch rocket systems firing extended range precision munitions against the mid-twentieth century main battle tanks and heavy artillery used by HAF. 

Logistics convoys and retreating HAF were subsequently hunted down and remotely engaged by the unmanned combat aerial vehicles or the lethal autonomous weapons systems such as the STM Kargu-2 (see annex 30) and other loitering munitions. The lethal autonomous weapons systems were programmed to attack targets without requiring data connectivity between the operator and the munition: in effect, a true ‘fire, forget and find’ capability. The unmanned combat aerial vehicles and the small drone intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance capability of HAF were neutralized by electronic jamming from the Koral electronic warfare system.

### Source C: The Story of America’s Very first Drone Strike 
In the autumn of 2001, however, the United States was unwilling to launch a full-scale land invasion in a region 7000 miles from home. Instead, a plan evolved to send into Afghanistan a small number of CIA agents and Special Forces in support of anti-Taliban militias, with the aid of the US Air Force. That first October night was a powerful display of coordination involving laser- guided munitions dropped from the air and Tomahawk cruise missiles launched from the sea. General Tommy Franks, who then led the US Central Command (CENTCOM), the military command overseeing operations in Afghanistan, wrote in his memoir American Soldier that the assault involved in total some 40,000 personnel, 393 aircraft, and 32 ships. But one aircraft did not feature at all in the Air Force’s complex planning: a tiny, CIA-controlled, propeller-driven spy drone, Predator tailfin number 3034 which had crept into Afghanistan some hours earlier. It now hangs suspended in the Smithsonian Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C., its place in history assured. Yet its actions that first night of the war – in which numerous agencies in the vast US military-intelligence machine each played sharply contradictory roles – remain steeped in controversy. 

### Source D 
Human Rights Watch released a report stating that representatives from around 50 countries will meet in the summer of 2021 at the UN to discuss worldwide policy alignment on ‘killer robots’ or ‘lethal autonomous weapons systems’. In their report, Human Rights Watch expressed objections to delegating lethal force to machines without the presence of meaningful human control. Bonnie Docherty, senior arms research at Human Rights Watch said: ‘The fundamental moral, legal and security concerns raised by autonomous weapons systems warrant a strong and urgent response in the form of a new international treaty ... International law needs to be expanded to create new rules that ensure human control and accountability in the use of force.’ Human Rights Watch proposes a treaty that covers the use of all weapons that operate autonomously that includes limitations and restrictions such as banning the use of killer robots, with many claims reinforcing that meaningful human control must be involved in the selection and engagement of targets. It goes on to define the scope and prevalence of ‘meaningful human control’ to ensure that humans have access to the data, risks and potential impacts prior to authorizing an attack.

### The Role of Firewalls in Network Security
Firewalls are critical for network security, acting as barriers between internal networks and external threats. They control incoming and outgoing traffic, protecting against unauthorized access and cyber attacks. However, configuring firewalls effectively can be challenging, especially in large organizations.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5b8dbedc-2d3a-4187-acb5-f7d90f2921b4)

parts.content: Evaluate the role of firewalls in securing organizational networks, considering their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation.
parts.markscheme:
Firewalls play a crucial role in securing organizational networks by acting as a barrier between trusted and untrusted networks, controlling the flow of traffic based on predefined security rules. Their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation can be evaluated as follows:

- Firewalls provide a first line of defense against unauthorized access and malicious attacks by:
  - Filtering incoming and outgoing network traffic based on IP addresses, ports, and protocols <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Blocking known malicious traffic and preventing unauthorized access to internal resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Enforcing security policies and access control lists (ACLs) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Effectiveness of firewalls:
  - They can effectively prevent many types of network-based attacks, such as distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, port scans, and unauthorized access attempts <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls can log and monitor network traffic, providing valuable information for security analysis and incident response <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - They can be configured to allow or deny specific types of traffic, providing granular control over network access <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Potential challenges in implementation:
  - Configuring and maintaining firewalls can be complex, especially in large organizations with diverse network architectures and security requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls may not be effective against advanced threats, such as zero-day attacks, encrypted traffic, or insider threats <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Improper configuration or rule management can lead to security vulnerabilities or unintended network disruptions <marks>1 mark</marks>
  <commonMistake>Relying solely on firewalls as the primary security measure, neglecting other security controls like intrusion detection/prevention systems, antivirus software, and user awareness training</commonMistake> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Discuss the importance of regularly updating firewall rules and software to address emerging threats and vulnerabilities</examTip> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of firewalls, their capabilities, limitations, and the need for a multi-layered security approach</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the challenges of implementing algorithmic transparency and accountability in criminal justice, particularly with “black box” algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Key Points

- Algorithmic transparency and accountability are important for ensuring fairness and due process in criminal justice systems
- "Black box" algorithms lack transparency, making it difficult to understand how decisions are made
- This can lead to biased or discriminatory outcomes, violating principles of justice

### Possible Arguments

- Lack of transparency means defendants/public cannot scrutinize algorithms for bias or errors <marks>1</marks>
- Opaque decision-making processes undermine due process and right to fair trial <marks>1</marks>
- Algorithms may perpetuate historical biases in data against minority groups <marks>1</marks>
- Proprietary algorithms used by private companies are not open to public audit <marks>1</marks>
- Difficulty in assigning accountability when errors/biases occur in complex systems <marks>1</marks>

### Counterarguments

- Algorithms can be more objective/consistent than human decision-making <marks>1</marks>
- Some privacy/security is needed to prevent gaming of algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Transparency could reveal sensitive information about individuals <marks>1</marks>
- Auditing/testing can validate algorithms without full transparency <marks>1</marks>

### Potential Solutions

- Regulatory oversight and auditing requirements for algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Open-sourcing algorithms for public scrutiny and accountability <marks>1</marks>
- Human oversight/ability to override algorithmic decisions <marks>1</marks>
- Ensuring representative and unbiased training data <marks>1</marks>

<note>Credit all relevant points from both sides. Maximum 12 marks.</note>

- Technical limitations and costs $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

  - Software development complexity for high-fidelity experiences

- Potential adverse physiological effects $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

  - Ergonomic issues from prolonged headset use

- Accessibility challenges $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Cost barriers for hardware/software adoption
  - Difficulties in adapting content for disabilities (e.g. vision, mobility)

questions.specification:
### Source A: Types of Computing Networks and Their Applications
Computing networks enable connectivity across different scales and purposes, from Local Area Networks (LANs) within buildings to Wide Area Networks (WANs) spanning cities. Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) enhance security, while Client-Server and Peer-to-Peer (P2P) architectures support various communication methods.

For instance, a VPN can secure remote employee access to a company’s intranet, and LANs connect devices within a corporate office to share resources efficiently.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7a3c6885-2c31-455f-b01e-7eb64e6d9ed2)

### Source B: Essential Components of Computing Networks
The effectiveness of networks depends on critical components such as clients, servers, routers, and switches. Modems connect devices to the internet, while network interfaces enable devices to join a network. These elements, together with wired and wireless media, make data transmission feasible across distances.

For example, a modem connects a home router to an internet service provider (ISP), enabling users to access the internet and local network resources.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d7a75ec2-df64-479b-af82-b9a6e19c859e)

### Source C: Transmission Media in Networks: Wired vs. Wireless
Networks rely on transmission media for data transfer. Wired media (e.g., Ethernet and fiber-optic cables) provide high-speed, reliable connections, while wireless media (e.g., Wi-Fi and cellular networks) enable mobility. Each medium has specific advantages depending on the application.

For instance, fiber-optic cables support high-speed internet in metropolitan areas, while Wi-Fi enables device connectivity within homes and offices without physical cabling.


### Source D: Network Protocols and Communication Rules
Protocols define communication standards for data exchange across networks. TCP/IP is foundational for the internet, while HTTP supports web browsing. Protocols like FTP and SMTP enable file transfers and email communication, respectively.

For example, TCP/IP underpins internet connections, ensuring data packets are transmitted between devices without data loss, even over long distances.

parts.content: With reference to Source A, identify two types of computing networks and describe one specific application for each.
parts.markscheme:
### Types of networks (award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each type identified, max <marks>2 marks</marks>):

- LAN (Local Area Network)
- WAN (Wide Area Network)
- PAN (Personal Area Network)
- MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)
- VPN (Virtual Private Network)

### Application for each type (award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each valid application described, max <marks>2 marks</marks>):

- LAN: Used to connect computers/devices within a limited area like an office, school or home to share resources like printers, files etc.
- WAN: Used to connect LANs over long distances, e.g. the internet which connects networks globally.
- PAN: Used for transferring data between devices in close proximity without an access point, e.g. Bluetooth connecting a phone to wireless headphones.
- MAN: Used to connect networks across an entire city/town, e.g. cable TV networks.
- VPN: Used to provide secure access to a private network over the internet, e.g. for remote employees to access company resources.

<note>Accept any other valid application. Do not award marks for just defining the network type.</note>

parts.content: With reference to Source B, explain the roles of a modem and a network interface in enabling network connectivity.
parts.markscheme:
### Roles of a modem

- Modulates (encodes) digital data from the computer into analog signals $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transmits the analog signals over telephone/cable lines $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Roles of a network interface

- Provides the physical connection between the computer and the network $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Handles the encoding/decoding of data into the appropriate format for transmission over the network $\triangleright$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: With reference to Source C, outline one advantage of using wired media and one advantage of using wireless media in networks.
parts.markscheme:
### Advantage of wired media
- More secure/less prone to interference/hacking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher bandwidth/faster data transfer rates <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Advantage of wireless media
- Mobility/portability/no cables required <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Easier to set up/install <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid advantage up to a maximum of 3 marks.</note>

parts.content: With reference to Source D, describe the function of the TCP/IP protocol in network communication.
parts.markscheme:
TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol) is a set of communication protocols used to transmit data over the Internet and other networks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Its main functions are:

- Establishing connections between devices/computers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Breaking data into packets and reassembling them at the destination
- Ensuring reliable data transmission by handling errors, retransmissions, and flow control

<note>No marks for simply stating that TCP/IP is a protocol or set of protocols without describing its function.</note>

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, discuss how network types, components, and protocols facilitate efficient data communication across different environments. Provide examples to support your answer.
parts.markscheme:
### Network Types <marks>2 marks</marks>

- Wired networks (e.g. Ethernet) provide high-speed, reliable data transfer over physical cables
  - Suitable for local area networks (LANs) within buildings/campuses
- Wireless networks (e.g. Wi-Fi, cellular) enable mobility and flexibility
  - Suitable for wide area networks (WANs) covering larger geographical areas

### Network Components <marks>4 marks</marks>

- Routers connect multiple networks and route data between them
  - Use routing protocols (e.g. OSPF, BGP) to determine optimal paths
- Switches connect devices within the same network
  - Use MAC addresses to forward data frames to the correct destination
- Access points provide wireless connectivity for client devices
- Servers store and distribute data/services across the network

### Network Protocols <marks>6 marks</marks>

- TCP/IP protocol suite governs data communication over the Internet
  - TCP ensures reliable, ordered delivery of data packets
  - IP handles logical addressing and routing of packets
- HTTP/HTTPS protocols enable retrieval of web resources
  - HTTP is unsecured, HTTPS uses encryption (e.g. TLS/SSL) for secure communication
- Email protocols like SMTP, POP3, IMAP facilitate email exchange
- VPN protocols (e.g. IPsec, SSL/TLS) create secure, encrypted connections over public networks

<examTip>Provide specific examples of how different network types, components, and protocols are used in various environments (e.g. home, office, mobile) to facilitate efficient data communication.</examTip>

### The Influence of AI on Political Power and Public Opinion
AI-driven algorithms on social media platforms play a significant role in shaping public opinion by curating political content and advertisements based on users' behavior. While this personalization can engage voters, it also raises concerns about the concentration of power in the hands of tech companies, the spread of misinformation, and the potential manipulation of public opinion.

parts.content: Evaluate the extent to which AI-driven content personalization influences political power dynamics, considering both the benefits of increased voter engagement and the risks of biased information and manipulation.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI's role in content personalization and its influence on public opinion.
- Limited evaluation of AI's influence on political power dynamics, with a predominantly descriptive approach.
- Few or no examples provided to substantiate points, with minimal reference to both benefits and risks.

Example Points:

- "AI algorithms personalize content, which can help people see more relevant political information."
- "However, this might lead to biased information that can influence people's views unfairly."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear understanding of how AI shapes political content on social media, with reference to both increased voter engagement and some risks (e.g., spread of biased information).
- Partial evaluation with an attempt to analyze the influence on political power dynamics, though analysis may lack depth or balance.
- Some examples provided to support points, with a general discussion on AI's role in engaging voters versus the risks of manipulation and misinformation.

Example Points:

- "AI algorithms increase engagement by tailoring content to users' interests, which can inform more people about political issues and potentially encourage greater voter turnout."
- "However, AI can create filter bubbles, isolating users within biased information silos and potentially allowing political manipulation by tech companies or political advertisers."
- Example: Platforms like Facebook and Twitter use AI to promote political content that aligns with a user's previous interactions, which can reinforce pre-existing biases and create echo chambers.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- Comprehensive evaluation of AI-driven personalization's influence on political power dynamics, with a balanced analysis of both increased engagement and critical risks like bias and manipulation.
- In-depth analysis of how AI consolidates power among tech companies, impacts voter behavior, and shapes public opinion by creating tailored information ecosystems. Detailed consideration of the ethical and democratic implications of such power.
- Well-chosen examples and insights into the consequences of AI-driven influence on political discourse and trust, and a nuanced view of how personalization might enhance democratic engagement but also risks undermining informed decision-making.

Example Points:

- "AI-driven personalization can boost civic engagement by showing users content that aligns with their interests and values, potentially mobilizing voters who might otherwise feel disengaged. Targeted content on issues like climate change or healthcare can empower users with relevant information, encouraging democratic participation."

Risks: "However, the personalization of political content also centralizes significant power in tech companies, which can manipulate public opinion by controlling what content users see. This influence may lead to echo chambers and filter bubbles, increasing polarization and limiting exposure to diverse perspectives, which are essential for informed decision-making."

Examples: "For instance, during election seasons, AI algorithms have been shown to promote certain narratives that align with users' beliefs, as seen with controversies around biased content on Facebook. The Cambridge Analytica case exemplifies the risk of misuse, where targeted ads influenced voter sentiment through hyper-personalized data points."

Conclusion: "While AI-driven personalization can encourage voter engagement, its potential to amplify bias and distort political discourse indicates the need for transparency and regulation. Balancing algorithmic benefits with ethical safeguards is essential for fostering a healthy democracy."

### Ethical Challenges of AI in Hiring and Recruitment
Many companies use AI-driven tools to screen job applicants and rank candidates. parts.content: To what extent should companies ensure fairness and transparency in AI-driven hiring processes, considering both the advantages of efficiency and the ethical risks of bias?
parts.markscheme:
Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>):
Basic understanding of AI in hiring, with minimal discussion on fairness and transparency.  
Limited or no evaluation of the ethical risks or efficiency benefits.
Little to no use of examples to illustrate points.

Example Points:
- "AI can make hiring more efficient by quickly screening applicants."
- "However, AI systems can introduce bias, affecting fairness."

Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>):
Clear understanding of the importance of fairness and transparency in AI-driven hiring.
Some analysis of both the efficiency benefits and ethical risks, although coverage may be imbalanced.
Examples provided to support points, but may lack detail or depth.

Example Points:
- "AI can streamline recruitment, allowing companies to process large numbers of applications quickly and objectively."
- "However, the lack of transparency in how these algorithms rank candidates can lead to biased decisions. For instance, if an algorithm is trained on past hiring data that reflects historical bias, it may disadvantage minority groups."
- Example: AI screening tools that penalize certain demographic markers or educational backgrounds.

Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>):
In-depth evaluation of the necessity for fairness and transparency in AI-driven hiring, fully addressing both the efficiency advantages and ethical risks.
Thorough analysis of the complexities and challenges in ensuring bias mitigation and accountability, with clear consideration of transparency.
Well-chosen examples used to illustrate the balance between efficiency and fairness, with potential solutions or recommendations discussed.

Example Points:
- "AI-driven recruitment tools allow companies to quickly filter applicants and identify key skills, improving efficiency in high-volume hiring processes. This can reduce costs and help to minimize certain human biases, as algorithms assess based on consistent criteria."
- "However, without transparency in how these tools operate, it is difficult to ensure fairness. For instance, Amazon's hiring algorithm was found to unintentionally penalize resumes with the word 'women,' highlighting how training data can propagate biases from past human decisions."
- Challenges: Ensuring fairness requires rigorous auditing and diverse training datasets, but these measures may reduce the efficiency benefits that AI offers. Legal and ethical accountability is another challenge, as it's often unclear who is responsible for the algorithm's biases.
- Solutions: To mitigate these risks, companies could implement regular audits and ensure transparency by publicly disclosing key aspects of how their algorithms evaluate candidates. Collaboration with external ethics boards or developing explainable AI (XAI) frameworks may help balance efficiency with fairness.

### Source A: The Transformation of Journalism Through Digital Media
Digital media has disrupted traditional journalism, leading to audience fragmentation and the consolidation of news organizations. While digital platforms extend news reach, they also challenge revenue models and credibility.

For example, while Twitter provides instant news updates, it has also led to challenges in verifying information, as content can be quickly shared without fact-checking.


### Source B: Media Authenticity and Deepfakes
Deepfake technology, enabled by AI, allows for realistic but fabricated videos, raising concerns about authenticity and trust. The growing prevalence of deepfakes calls for new ways to verify digital content.

For example, deepfakes can create fabricated political speeches, potentially influencing public opinion through misinformation.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/526866e2-518b-4ead-a0f5-48d92d554307)

### Source C: Ownership, Copyright, and Remix Culture in Digital Media
Digital media has encouraged a remix culture, where creators can adapt existing content. However, this has brought up issues around copyright, ownership, and the need for Creative Commons licenses to legally share work.

For instance, remixes of popular songs or videos must comply with copyright laws, unless distributed under licenses that allow adaptations.

### Source D: Media Obsolescence and Digital Preservation
Digital content can quickly become obsolete, leading to media obsolescence and necessitating digital preservation. Websites and digital files often become inaccessible without updates or archiving efforts.

For example, digital archives like the Internet Archive preserve web content, ensuring continued access to valuable digital information.

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Black Box Problem: Algorithmic Transparency and Accountability
Many complex algorithms, particularly those used in machine learning, operate as ""black boxes,"" meaning their decision-making processes are not easily understood. This lack of transparency and accountability raises ethical concerns, especially when algorithms impact individuals' lives in areas like credit scoring or criminal justice.

For example, an algorithm used for credit scoring may make decisions without revealing the factors it considered, leading to a lack of accountability.


### Source B: Erosion of Human Judgment in Algorithmic Decision-Making
The rise of algorithmic decision-making can lead to a loss of human judgment, especially in areas where subjective understanding is crucial, like healthcare or social services. Over-reliance on algorithms may result in decisions that overlook nuanced human factors.

For instance, a diagnostic algorithm in healthcare might misinterpret symptoms that a human physician could recognize due to experience or intuition.


### Source C: Algorithm Efficiency: Measuring Time and Space Complexity
Algorithm efficiency is critical, especially in processing large datasets. Efficiency is measured by time complexity (how fast an algorithm runs) and space complexity (how much memory it uses). Balancing these aspects is essential in applications that require speed and performance, like data processing in finance.

For example, financial trading algorithms are optimized to make quick, efficient decisions based on market data to execute trades faster than human traders.


### Source D: Algorithmic Filtering and Prioritization in Social Media
Social media platforms use algorithms to filter and prioritize content, often shaping users' experiences and the information they see. These algorithms analyze user data to decide which posts appear first, influencing what trends, news, and advertisements are visible to each user.

For example, a social media algorithm might prioritize posts from close contacts and trending news topics to keep users engaged.

parts.content:
**A cashless society**

In the near future, it is possible that cash will not be accepted as a means of payment in Sweden. People are already using alternative ways of paying, such as mobile payment, card payment and internet payment. Currently, over 95% of citizens in Sweden have internet access.

Many people in Sweden claim there are advantages of using an app developed by Swish. The Swish app allows friends to share a restaurant bill, pay where credit or debit cards are not accepted, for babysitting or parking tickets, or make a donation at church.

However, other people in Sweden claim that making the Swish app the only means of payment may increase inequalities within the country.

Discuss whether countries should pass legislation making apps such as Swish the only means of payment.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Benefits of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (claim)**

*   No need to carry cash (digitalization) / credit cards – no risk of being stolen/no risk of not having enough cash.
*   No risk of credit card being used fraudulently (values).
*   Transactions are recorded – there is proof of payment, and the system should increase transparency / acceptability.
*   Payments are made immediately – no need to wait until person has time to go to bank to get cash (feasibility).
*   Can solve other problems regarding money – bills can be shared, one person pays, and money is transferred (feasibility).
*   In an emergency, money can be transferred to dependents without them being close (e.g., children at university) (feasibility).
*   Allows money transactions between individuals.
*   Can limit the amount of money to be transferred, thus preventing individuals spending more than they have available (acceptability / feasibility).
*   Many people are already used to the app, so it would be a good choice if the country were going cashless (acceptability).
*   Easier for users to track budgets / spending, as all transactions are digital (transparency).

**Disadvantages of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (counter-claim)**

*   Swedish banks will be able to obtain more data on their users’ transaction habits (privacy concern, ethics, values).
*   Is not available to people who do not have a bank account, so a potential digital divide concern (equity).
*   May be problematic for tourists who may not have the app or who cannot link the purchase to their bank account (systems, equity).
*   Removes the anonymity of the payee in transactions – the app may store a user’s transaction history. This would include date, item, recipient of the money and cost of the item (values, ethics).
*   The bank controls (power) the maximum amount of money that can be transferred, which may limit a person’s spending and may not be appropriate in certain situations (acceptability).
*   It may not be technically possible to make the transition from a society that uses cash for transactions to one that does not (change, systems).
*   If a single app is used, it would give Swish an unfair monopoly over the technology (power, values, ethics).
*   Digital divide – smartphone ownership and use by mature adults.
*   If a person loses their phone, breaks it, or its battery runs out, they have no way to pay for anything (systems, equity).
*   Failure in / lack of phone network coverage could affect when and where people could use the app (systems, equity).
*   Failure of the system / technical issues / down time would prevent people from making transactions (systems, feasibility).

_Accept implicit and explicit references to apps such as the Swish app._

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** business, families, digital divide, access, inclusion, acceptability, feasibility, equity, digitalization, anonymity, privacy, change, power, systems, values, ethics

parts.content: Identify two ethical dilemmas associated with AI and describe their implications.
parts.markscheme:
### Ethical Dilemma 1: Bias and Discrimination

AI systems can perpetuate and amplify societal biases present in the training data, leading to unfair and discriminatory outcomes. This can result in:
- Biased decision-making in areas like hiring, lending, and criminal justice
- Reinforcing existing prejudices and stereotypes
- Disproportionately disadvantaging certain groups

### Ethical Dilemma 2: Privacy and Surveillance

The massive data collection and processing capabilities of AI raise privacy concerns:
- Potential for unauthorized access, misuse, or exploitation of personal data
- Erosion of individual privacy through pervasive surveillance and monitoring
- Lack of transparency and control over how personal data is used

### Ethical Dilemma 3: Accountability and Transparency

AI systems can be opaque "black boxes," making it difficult to understand their decision-making processes:
- Lack of accountability for errors or harmful outcomes
- Difficulty in assigning responsibility and liability
- Challenges in ensuring fairness, explainability, and due process

### Ethical Dilemma 4: Automation and Job Displacement

The increasing automation enabled by AI could lead to significant job losses and economic disruption:
- Potential for mass unemployment and income inequality
- Challenges in retraining and reskilling the workforce
- Societal and economic impacts on individuals and communities

<marks>2 marks for identifying and describing any two ethical dilemmas associated with AI</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the ethical challenges of automation in AI, particularly regarding job displacement and economic impacts.
parts.markscheme:
### Key Points
- Automation and AI can lead to job displacement and economic disruption <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - As AI systems become more capable, they may replace human workers in various industries
  - This could result in job losses and economic hardship for those displaced
- Potential negative economic impacts <marks>2 marks</marks>

### Ethical Considerations

questions.specification:
**Source A**  


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/28a20f99-1da6-411b-b2ce-d06dc46e7256)


**Source B (excerpt from Maple Tower’s website)**  


Maple Tower Apartments adopted HomeGate, a resident app linked to smart locks in the lobby and parcel room. Residents can issue time-limited digital keys to guests, generate QR codes for deliveries, and receive notifications when parcels are scanned into the building. HomeGate also includes a neighbour feed for announcements and an incident report function that allows residents to upload photos and select categories such as noise, suspicious behaviour, smoking, or property damage. The building manager states that the system reduces lost packages and helps staff respond faster to complaints. HomeGate stores access logs, including door unlock events and QR scans, for 30 days, and residents may request staff review during disputes. Some residents report feeling safer, while others argue the app encourages excessive reporting and makes casual interactions in shared spaces feel monitored.


**Source C**  


After HomeGate was introduced, reported package losses fell from 14 per month to 4 per month. Incident reports rose from 6 per month to 31 per month in the first two months, then stabilised at 18 per month. Twenty-seven percent of all reports were later marked unfounded or unverifiable. Delivery workers accounted for 41 percent of reports labelled suspicious, despite spending limited time in the building. A resident survey found that 48 percent talk to neighbours less in the lobby, while 35 percent say the neighbour feed helps them feel more connected. Complaints about profiling increased, especially from residents hosting caregivers or receiving medical deliveries.


**Source D (commentary from an anonymous Maple Tower resident)**  


HomeGate is presented as community technology, but it can quietly transform social life into a security workflow. When every visitor has a code and every door creates a log, suspicion becomes easier and empathy less necessary. The data show fewer lost packages but also more reporting and many unfounded accusations. Those most often labelled suspicious are delivery workers and caregivers, who already hold low-status positions. The system does not create prejudice, but it can accelerate it by turning feelings into categories and categories into records. Even a 30-day access log can change behaviour, discouraging informal help or spontaneous interaction. A residential building is a social system, not only a set of access points, and safety measures should avoid damaging community trust.

**Analyse (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

  
* Increased monitoring through access logs and incident reporting makes shared spaces feel monitored, which reduces casual interaction in common areas <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The incident report feature can encourage more complaints, potentially increasing suspicion and neighbour conflict rather than resolving underlying issues <marks>1 mark</marks>

* Package loss reduction may reduce some conflicts over missing deliveries but creates new tensions around who is monitored and categorised <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Accept responses that reference either Source B or Source C, or both. Award marks only when students move beyond listing features and explain how those features change social relationships.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
HomeGate may change social relationships through several mechanisms. Access logs and incident reporting make shared spaces feel monitored, which aligns with survey data showing that many residents talk less to neighbours in the lobby.  

  
The incident report feature can encourage more complaints, potentially increasing suspicion and conflict rather than resolving underlying tensions. At the same time, the neighbour feed can increase a sense of connection for some residents by providing a space for announcements and updates. The suspicious behaviour category may also concentrate suspicion on delivery workers and caregivers, which can undermine inclusion and trust within the community.

parts.content: Compare what Source C and Source D suggest about HomeGate’s impact on inclusion and suspicion in the community.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

  
* Both Source C and Source D show that HomeGate increases suspicion through high volumes of reporting and unfounded accusations <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C provides quantitative evidence that 27 percent of reports are unfounded, while Source D frames this as a system that turns feelings into categories and records <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C shows that delivery workers account for 41 percent of reports labelled suspicious, while Source D argues this reflects accelerated prejudice against low status workers <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source C reports mixed inclusion outcomes, with fewer lobby conversations but some increased connection via the neighbour feed <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Source D emphasises chilling effects on informal social interaction, suggesting that logging and categorisation reduce spontaneous help and greetings <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Both sources acknowledge practical benefits such as reduced package loss but raise concerns about costs to community trust and social cohesion <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for each effective comparison grounded in both Source C and Source D.  
Maximum award is <marks>6 marks</marks>.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses using only one source should be limited. Strong answers directly compare similarities or differences in perspective between the sources.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
Source C and Source D both suggest that HomeGate increases suspicion in the community. Source C provides evidence that a significant proportion of reports are unfounded, which supports Source D’s argument that the system turns feelings into formal categories and records.  

  
Source C shows that delivery workers account for a large share of suspicious reports, while Source D interprets this as an acceleration of prejudice against low status workers. Source C presents mixed inclusion outcomes, whereas Source D stresses chilling effects on informal interaction. Both sources recognise reduced package loss but argue that these benefits come at the cost of community trust.

parts.content: Examine whether HomeGate improves or harms the social life of Maple Tower. With reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course, consider surveillance and social trust, digital divides in participation, power dynamics between residents/staff/service workers, and how design choices shape community norms.
parts.markscheme:
**Examine (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

  
* Reduced package loss from 14 to 4 per month can reduce conflict and increase perceived safety for residents <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Faster complaint response and clearer dispute resolution through access logs can reduce uncertainty about building security <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The neighbour feed can support community connection for some residents, as shown by 35 percent reporting increased connection <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Access logs and incident reporting make interactions feel monitored, reducing lobby conversations and spontaneous social interaction <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* High reporting volumes with 27 percent unfounded reports and disproportionate tagging of delivery workers support claims of bias acceleration <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Residents gain reporting power over staff and service workers, shifting power dynamics within the building <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Service workers and caregivers may be exposed to categorisation and lasting records, affecting working relationships <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Residents who do not use the app may have less voice in announcements or dispute processes <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Surveillance created by 30 day access logs can produce chilling effects on trust and informal exchange <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Predefined reporting categories shape what residents notice and define as suspicious behaviour <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Data retention policies influence behaviour by making interactions feel traceable and permanent <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Platform based neighbourhood governance shifts power and accountability in residential communities <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>12 marks</marks> for a balanced examination that addresses both improvements and harms to social life.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>To reach full marks, responses should synthesise source evidence with relevant own knowledge, consider surveillance, digital divides, power dynamics, and design choices, and reach a supported judgement.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
HomeGate produces mixed effects on the social life of Maple Tower. On the positive side, reduced package loss from 14 to 4 per month can decrease conflict and increase residents’ sense of safety. Faster complaint handling and clearer dispute resolution through access logs can also reduce uncertainty about building security. The neighbour feed provides a space for announcements and updates, and some residents report feeling more connected as a result.  

  
However, these benefits are accompanied by social costs. Access logs and incident reporting make shared spaces feel monitored, which is associated with fewer lobby conversations and reduced spontaneous interaction. A high volume of unfounded reports and the disproportionate tagging of delivery workers show how design choices can accelerate bias by turning suspicion into formal records. Reporting tools also shift power dynamics by giving residents greater oversight of staff and service workers, potentially undermining trust.  

  
Residents who do not use the app may have less voice in community life, reflecting digital divides. Overall, while HomeGate improves efficiency and security, it risks weakening informal social relationships and community trust unless surveillance features and reporting systems are carefully limited and supported by inclusive design choices.

parts.content: Outline the role of network protocols, such as TCP/IP, in enabling data exchange.
Explain how routers and switches work together to support connectivity within a network.
parts.markscheme:
- Routers connect multiple networks together and route data packets between them <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Switches connect devices within the same network and forward data packets to the intended destination <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Routers:} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Operate at the network layer (layer 3) of the OSI model} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Use IP addresses to determine where to forward packets} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Connect different networks (e.g., LAN to WAN)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Switches:} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Operate at the data link layer (layer 2) of the OSI model} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Use MAC addresses to forward packets within the same network} \\
&\bullet\: \text{Connect devices in the same LAN}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Routers and switches work together to enable communication across networks <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Switches connect devices within a LAN
  - Routers connect LANs to other networks (e.g., the internet)

parts.content: Evaluate the benefits and challenges of using wireless transmission media over wired media in a corporate network.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefits of wireless transmission media

- Mobility/portability <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Users can move around freely with wireless devices
  - Devices are not tethered to a fixed location

- Ease of installation/deployment <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - No need to install cables/wires
  - Wireless networks can be set up quickly

- Cost savings <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Eliminates cabling costs
  - Reduces labor costs for installation

- Scalability/flexibility <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Easy to expand wireless network coverage
  - Can accommodate changes in office layout/environment

### Challenges of wireless transmission media

- Security risks <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Wireless signals can be intercepted/eavesdropped
  - Vulnerable to unauthorized access/hacking

- Interference/signal degradation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Signals can be disrupted by obstacles, other wireless devices
  - Limited range/coverage compared to wired networks

- Bandwidth limitations <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Wireless networks generally have lower bandwidth than wired
  - May not be suitable for high-bandwidth applications

- Compliance/regulatory issues <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Need to follow wireless regulations/standards
  - Potential legal issues with unauthorized wireless access points

Examples of valid responses:
- Paintings/drawings/sculptures by living artists
- Photographs taken by living photographers

<note>Do not award marks for generic responses like "artwork" or "creative works" without specifying a valid example.</note>

parts.content: Explain two limitations of copyright laws
parts.markscheme:
Limitations of copyright laws:

- Copyright laws only protect the expression of ideas, not the ideas themselves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Anyone can use the underlying ideas, concepts, or principles expressed in a copyrighted work.

- Copyright protection is limited in duration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright eventually expires, and works enter the public domain, allowing free use.
  - Example: In most countries, copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 50-70 years.

- Fair use/fair dealing exceptions allow limited use of copyrighted works without permission. <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Examples: Criticism, commentary, news reporting, research, teaching, etc.
  - The amount used must be reasonable and not harm the market for the work.

- Copyright laws have limitations on enforcement across borders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright is territorial, and laws vary between countries.
  - Enforcing copyright internationally can be challenging.

<note>Award 1 mark for each well-explained limitation, up to a maximum of 6 marks.</note>

parts.content: The evolution of the internet and computing has made it easier for people to illegally copy and distribute works of art. Discuss the impact this is having on the music industry.
parts.markscheme:
Some points could include: 1. Loss of Revenue: Illegal downloading and sharing of music files lead to a reduction in revenue for artists, record labels, and other stakeholders.
With easier access to pirated music, consumers may choose not to pay for songs or albums, directly affecting sales and reducing income for the music industry. 2. Challenges for Emerging Artists: New artists face difficulties in establishing themselves due to the loss of potential revenue streams.
They rely on album sales and streaming royalties to fund their careers, and piracy undermines these opportunities, making it harder to sustain a livelihood solely through music. 3. Shift in Business Models: The industry has had to adapt, with a shift toward streaming services (e.g., Spotify, Apple Music) as a way to counteract piracy.
While these services help reduce illegal downloads, they often provide lower per-stream revenue for artists compared to traditional album sales. 4. Impact on Creativity and Production Quality: Lower revenue may mean fewer resources for high-quality production, as smaller labels and independent artists have less to invest in their music.
This can limit creative exploration and reduce the overall quality and variety of music available to consumers. 5. Increased Reliance on Live Performances and Merchandise: To counterbalance losses from album sales, artists increasingly rely on live performances, tours, and merchandise as primary revenue sources.
However, live performances are not feasible for all artists, especially for those with physical or logistical limitations, meaning they are more severely impacted by illegal distribution. 6. Legal and Ethical Concerns: Piracy raises ethical issues, as it undermines artists' rights to their own work.
The music industry has implemented stricter copyright laws and taken legal action against file-sharing platforms, but enforcement remains challenging due to the global nature of the internet. 7. Potential Positive Exposure: On a positive note, free access to music can increase exposure for some artists, allowing them to reach a wider audience and potentially gain fans who may later pay for concert tickets or official merchandise.
However, this benefit is uncertain and varies widely depending on the artist's popularity and audience. Conclusion: The illegal copying and distribution of music have forced the industry to adapt, with mixed results. While streaming and alternative revenue sources provide some compensation, piracy continues to pose financial and ethical challenges, particularly for smaller artists. The industry must balance between protecting intellectual property and embracing new digital business models that meet changing consumer behaviors. 8 marks Valid points should be accepted with 1 mark for the point and an additional point for a supporting explanation/example.

A conditional is a statement in the algorithm that executes actions based on specific criteria (e.g., if an obstacle is detected, the car stops).

$$
\text{if (obstacle detected):} \\

$$

parts.content: Identify two reasons why variables are critical for self-driving car algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Identify two reasons why variables are critical for self-driving car algorithms. <marks>2 marks</marks>

1. Variables are used to store and manipulate data from sensors (e.g., distance to obstacles, speed, location) that is essential for the algorithm to make decisions.

2. Variables allow the algorithm to keep track of the car's state (e.g., current speed, direction) and adjust its behavior accordingly.

<commonMistake>
Failing to recognize that variables are necessary to store and process dynamic data from the car's environment and internal state.
</commonMistake>

parts.content: Explain how loops improve efficiency in a self-driving car's algorithm for real-time monitoring.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>4 marks</marks>

Loops allow continuous monitoring of road conditions by repeatedly checking sensors, enabling quick responses to changes without repeating code manually.

$$
\text{while (car is running):} \\
\quad \text{check sensors} \\
\quad \text{update car state}
$$

parts.content: Discuss the challenges of creating self-driving algorithms without using variables or conditionals.
parts.markscheme:
### Discuss the challenges of creating self-driving algorithms without using variables or conditionals.

Without variables:
- It would be impossible to store and process data from sensors (e.g., distance to obstacles, speed, location) <marks>2 marks</marks>
- The algorithm could not keep track of the car's state (e.g., current speed, direction) or adjust its behavior accordingly <marks>2 marks</marks>

Without conditionals:
- The algorithm would lack the ability to make decisions based on specific criteria or situations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- It could not adapt its behavior based on changing road conditions or obstacles, leading to potential safety issues <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, focusing on the balance between economic efficiency and potential job displacement.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI-driven automation and its general effect on employment.
- Limited discussion on economic efficiency or job displacement, with little depth or balance between the two.
- Few examples or specifics to illustrate key points.


"AI-driven automation can lead to job loss in some industries."
"Automation can make work faster and more efficient."
</commonMistake>

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of AI-driven automation's dual impact on employment, including both potential economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Some discussion of benefits (e.g., improved productivity and cost reduction) alongside basic mention of risks (e.g., job loss in specific sectors).
- Examples provided to support points, though may lack depth or variety.


"AI-driven automation increases economic efficiency by speeding up processes and reducing costs, which benefits businesses."
"However, certain jobs, especially in manufacturing and repetitive tasks, may be at risk of displacement."
Examples might include references to automation in retail (self-checkout systems) or manufacturing (robotic assembly lines).
</commonMistake>

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, considering both economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Balanced analysis of benefits and risks, showing nuanced understanding of how automation can drive economic growth but also result in challenges for workforce adaptation.
- Detailed examination of sectors where automation is most impactful (e.g., manufacturing, retail, transportation) and where new opportunities may emerge (e.g., tech support, AI maintenance).
- Examples used effectively to illustrate points, with insights into both the positive impacts (e.g., efficiency, growth in new sectors) and negative impacts (e.g., job displacement, need for reskilling).

$$
\text{Economic Efficiency} \leftrightarrow \text{Job Displacement}
$$


"AI-driven automation enhances economic efficiency by reducing labor costs and increasing productivity, allowing companies to produce goods and services at lower prices, potentially increasing consumer access."
"However, automation can displace jobs in sectors where repetitive tasks are common. For instance, autonomous vehicles could reduce the demand for truck drivers, and robotic process automation could decrease the need for administrative roles."
"There is also a shift in job creation, with new roles emerging in AI development, maintenance, and oversight, but this requires significant retraining and may not offset losses in low-skill jobs."
</commonMistake>

<examTip>Balancing these effects is difficult, as job displacement impacts socio-economic stability. Policymakers might need to consider interventions such as retraining programs, universal basic income, or support for sectors heavily affected by automation.</examTip>

parts.content: To what extent does bandwidth availability impact the quality of online activities like streaming and gaming, considering both user experience and network limitations?
parts.markscheme:
- Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection in a given time period <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher bandwidth allows for faster data transfer rates and better quality for online activities like:
    - Video streaming (higher resolution, less buffering) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Online gaming (lower latency, smoother gameplay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Video conferencing (higher quality audio/video) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- User experience is impacted by bandwidth limitations:
    - Low bandwidth leads to poor quality video/audio, lag, buffering <marks>1 mark</marks>
    <commonMistake>Confusing bandwidth with internet speed</commonMistake>

- Network limitations that impact bandwidth availability:
    - Type of internet connection (e.g. fiber optic vs DSL) <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Distance from internet service provider <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Network congestion during peak usage times <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Shared bandwidth among multiple users/devices <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Solutions to increase bandwidth:
    - Upgrade to a higher-tier internet plan <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Use wired Ethernet instead of WiFi <marks>1 mark</marks>
    - Optimize home network (e.g. better router placement) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Answers should consider both the user perspective and technical network factors.</note>

parts.content: Outline how ANNs support natural language processing in chatbots.
parts.markscheme:
ANNs support natural language processing (NLP) in chatbots in the following ways:

- ANNs can be trained on large datasets of human language to recognize patterns and extract meaning from text input. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They can analyze the syntax, semantics, and context of user queries to understand the intent behind the message. <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\text{For example, an ANN can differentiate between "What is the weather today?"}
$$
$$
\text{and "What is the weather like in London today?"}
$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

- ANNs can generate relevant responses by mapping the interpreted input to appropriate output sequences, mimicking human-like conversation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Maximum 4 marks</note>

$$

$$

parts.content: Evaluate the impact of artificial neural networks on safety and reliability in autonomous vehicles, considering both benefits and challenges.
parts.markscheme:
### Benefits

- ANNs can process large amounts of visual data in real-time, allowing for rapid detection of obstacles, pedestrians, and other vehicles on the road. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ANNs can be trained on diverse datasets, improving their ability to recognize objects in various conditions (lighting, weather, etc.). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ANNs can continuously learn and adapt, potentially improving safety as more data is collected. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Challenges

- ANNs can be susceptible to adversarial attacks or unexpected inputs, leading to misclassifications that could compromise safety. <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$
\text{Example: Stickers on a stop sign could cause it to be misclassified}
$$

- ANNs are "black boxes," making it difficult to understand their decision-making process, which raises concerns about reliability and accountability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- ANNs require large amounts of training data, which can be expensive and time-consuming to collect and label. <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Incomplete or biased data can lead to safety issues.</note>

<commonMistake>
Failing to consider both benefits and challenges, or only discussing one aspect (safety or reliability).
</commonMistake>

<examTip>
Provide specific examples to illustrate the benefits and challenges of using ANNs in autonomous vehicles.
</examTip>

### AI and Transparency in Decision-Making
AI is used in critical areas such as finance, healthcare, and criminal justice, where decision-making transparency is crucial. ""Black box"" algorithms, however, lack transparency, making it difficult for users to understand or challenge AI-driven decisions, which can affect trust and fairness.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ec4096a8-1afd-4152-9af1-bae97f68f6a8)

parts.content: To what extent should transparency be a priority in AI-driven decision-making, particularly in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice?
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of transparency in AI.
- Limited discussion on why transparency might be important, with minimal reference to high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice.
- Few or no examples to support points.

Example Points:

- "Transparency is needed to see how AI decisions are made."
- "It is important in high-stakes areas like finance."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of the concept of transparency and its importance in AI decision-making.
- Some discussion of high-stakes areas (finance, criminal justice), mentioning the potential consequences of opaque AI systems.
- Basic mention of risks in not prioritizing transparency, such as accountability and fairness issues.
- Some examples to illustrate key points, though may lack depth.

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI is crucial for accountability, especially in areas like criminal justice where lives can be directly affected."
- "In finance, a lack of transparency could lead to unfair credit decisions, impacting people's financial stability."
- Examples may include basic references to credit scoring or criminal sentencing decisions influenced by AI without deep analysis.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the necessity of transparency in AI, particularly in high-stakes fields.
- Comprehensive discussion of ethical and practical implications, including how transparency supports accountability, trust, and fairness.
- Detailed analysis of risks associated with opaque AI systems, such as potential bias, wrongful decisions, and the challenges to correcting errors without transparency.
- Balanced argument with consideration of challenges to implementing transparency, like trade-offs with proprietary information or the complexity of AI models.
- Use of specific examples to illustrate implications in both finance (e.g., credit scoring or loan approvals) and criminal justice (e.g., sentencing algorithms or predictive policing).

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI decision-making is essential in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice, where decisions have far-reaching impacts on individuals' lives. It ensures that affected parties can understand, contest, and seek recourse for decisions that impact them."
- "In criminal justice, lack of transparency in AI-driven sentencing algorithms can lead to biased outcomes without accountability, eroding public trust. Transparency is needed to scrutinize whether the system perpetuates existing biases, particularly against marginalized groups."
- "In finance, opaque algorithms used in credit scoring can disadvantage certain applicants without providing a clear reason, limiting access to financial opportunities."
- <note>Challenges: Achieving transparency can be difficult when models are complex or proprietary, but efforts like explainable AI and regulatory oversight could help balance transparency with innovation.</note>

parts.content: Outline the concept of the uncanny valley and its effect on user acceptance.
parts.markscheme:
The uncanny valley refers to the phenomenon where robots or computer animations that appear almost human can cause feelings of unease or revulsion in observers. As the appearance and motion of robots become more human-like, observers' emotional response becomes increasingly positive.
However, at a point where the robot/animation is close to being human-like, but not quite there, observers experience an unsettling feeling, causing their emotional response to drop sharply.
<marks>2 marks</marks>

This effect can negatively impact user acceptance of robots in roles that require close human interaction, such as customer service or healthcare. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Clearly explain the concept of the uncanny valley and its impact on user acceptance to score full marks.</examTip>
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how autonomous technologies can lead to job displacement in sectors like manufacturing.
parts.markscheme:
### Autonomous technologies leading to job displacement in manufacturing

- Autonomous technologies like robots and automated systems can perform many manufacturing tasks more efficiently and consistently than human workers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This reduces the need for human labor in manufacturing processes <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\text{As automation increases} \Rightarrow \text{fewer human workers required}
$$

- Entire manufacturing lines can be automated, displacing large numbers of workers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- <examTip>Give specific examples of manufacturing tasks that can be automated, e.g. assembly, welding, packaging</examTip>
- <note>Maximum 3 marks if no link is made to job displacement</note>

<marks>4 marks total</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the social and economic impacts of job displacement due to robots, considering both productivity gains and the need for workforce adaptation.
parts.markscheme:
### Potential Positive Impacts

- Increased productivity and economic growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Robots can work 24/7 without breaks, increasing output
  - Lower labor costs can make businesses more competitive

- Creation of new job opportunities <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - New roles in robot maintenance, programming, etc.
  - Workforce can transition to emerging tech sectors

### Potential Negative Impacts

- Job losses and unemployment <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Robots can replace human workers in manufacturing, logistics, etc.
  - Leads to economic hardship for displaced workers

- Inequality and wage stagnation <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Workers with skills complementary to robots benefit
  - But low-skill workers face competition, downward pressure on wages

- Social unrest and resistance <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Fears of job insecurity can fuel backlash against automation
  - Calls for policies to protect human labor

### Workforce Adaptation Needs

- Reskilling and retraining programs <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Equip workers with skills for new roles in tech-driven economy
  - Promote lifelong learning and adaptability

- Social safety nets and transition assistance <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Income support, job placement services for displaced workers
  - Facilitate workforce mobility across sectors

<note>Candidates should consider both economic benefits of productivity gains as well as social costs of job displacement. A balanced evaluation weighing pros and cons is expected for full marks.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the ethical implications of user data collection and surveillance on social networks, focusing on privacy, security, and consent.
parts.markscheme:
### Privacy and consent <marks>4 marks</marks>
- User data collection often happens without explicit consent or transparency
- Privacy concerns over personal data being collected, shared, and used for purposes not intended by users
- Lack of control over how personal data is used and disseminated
- Potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information

### Security risks <marks>3 marks</marks>
- Collection of user data creates security vulnerabilities and risks of hacking/data breaches
- Centralized data storage makes platforms an attractive target for cyber attacks
- Stolen data can be used for identity theft, fraud, or other malicious activities
$$
\text{Risk} \propto \text{Amount of data collected}
$$

### Surveillance and social control <marks>3 marks</marks>
- User data enables mass surveillance and monitoring of online activities
- Potential for data to be used for social control, manipulation, or discrimination
- Chilling effects on free speech and expression due to perceived monitoring
- Asymmetry of power between platforms with user data and individual users

### Ethical considerations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Lack of transparency and informed consent violates ethical principles
- User autonomy and right to privacy are undermined
- Utilitarian arguments about benefits must be weighed against ethical costs
- Need for stronger data protection laws and user-centric data governance models
